conflicts.description: The previous sheet fixed the book in mid-September, but the manuscript repeatedly describes late-September or near-October light while only firmly anchoring the week as a Thursday-to-Wednesday September draft before a Thursday season opener.
conflicts.canonical_value: September 2024 draft week, a few days before the Thursday season opener; exact week left unspecified.
conflicts.resolution_note: The old sheet's mid-September precision is unsupported. Repeated atmosphere cues push later in September, but the manuscript and context pack only securely support 'September,' so the updated sheet generalizes instead of forcing an exact week.

conflicts.description: The previous sheet said Martin inherited the lake house from his parents, but the manuscript says his parents bought it and Martin kept it in the divorce five years before the story because Linda did not contest it.
conflicts.canonical_value: Martin kept the lake house in the divorce five years before the story; his parents originally bought it in the 1980s.
conflicts.resolution_note: The manuscript is explicit and the earlier sheet wording is not supported elsewhere, so this reads as an early-sheet mistake rather than later manuscript drift.

conflicts.description: Chapter 2 shows Paul first seeing Martin already on his back, but later investigative chapters say Paul originally found him on his left side facing the window and then rolled him during CPR.
conflicts.canonical_value: Paul originally found Martin on his left side facing the window before moving him during CPR.
conflicts.resolution_note: The left-side version is repeated in the interview and later deduction work, so it is more deeply embedded in the book's causal structure than the isolated Chapter 2 description.

conflicts.description: The manuscript gives three materially different versions of the 2002 RT incident: Chapter 8 has an August 17 account about an August 11 cabin fire caused by a knocked-over kerosene heater; Chapter 16 has an August 14 account involving gasoline and a shed or space heater; Chapter 17 has Tommy describing a dropped cigarette near a propane line.
conflicts.canonical_value: Only the shared overlap is canon: in August 2002, a Washburn County fire killed caretaker Ray Toomey; Tommy caused it through drunk carelessness; Martin helped bury the truth and kept a private RT account in the safe.
conflicts.resolution_note: All three versions are causally important but incompatible on specifics. The updated sheet keeps only the overlap, which is also the level supported by the cycle context pack, instead of inventing a single exact mechanism.

conflicts.description: Chapter 8 has Cliff counting roughly $2,000 cash in the safe, while Chapter 11 has Paul counting $860 in twenties and fifties from the same safe.
conflicts.canonical_value: The safe contains some cash; the updated sheet leaves the amount unspecified.
conflicts.resolution_note: The manuscript never explains the discrepancy, and the amount is not plot-critical. The updated sheet keeps the stable fact that cash is present without inventing missing theft, recount error, or another off-page event.

conflicts.description: Doug's post-divorce apartment is described as Richfield in Chapter 7, but Chapter 14 says he moved into a one-bedroom in Burnsville after leaving Roseville.
conflicts.canonical_value: Doug is living alone in a south-metro Twin Cities apartment after the divorce; city left unspecified.
conflicts.resolution_note: The city is non-central and internally inconsistent, so the updated sheet keeps only the stable continuity that Doug is living alone after the separation.

conflicts.description: Doug first sees Cliff with a gash on the right temple, but the later police and Mallick descriptions place the fatal head wound on the left side or left temple.
conflicts.canonical_value: Cliff suffered a temple head wound before drowning; the updated sheet leaves the side unspecified.
conflicts.resolution_note: Later forensic chapters repeat the left-side version, but the manuscript conflicts on laterality and the exact side is not needed for downstream continuity, so the sheet stays broader.

conflicts.description: Chapter 1 establishes a seven-man board with Martin drawing the final slot and circling his own picks in red on Thursday night, but Chapter 18 describes twelve columns, puts Martin fourth from the left, and treats the red circles as something one of the men may have added after his death.
conflicts.canonical_value: Seven-man board for the seven friends; Martin drafted from the final slot and circled his own picks in red on Thursday night.
conflicts.resolution_note: The opening draft scene and the cycle premise both structurally depend on a seven-man league. Chapter 18's board description reads as late-stage manuscript drift.
